title: Dog Green Discharge from Prepuce: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Discover what causes green discharge from a dog's prepuce. Learn about smegma, urgency levels, and why triage photos are helpful for diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Green or yellowish discharge from a male dog's prepuce is usually normal smegma and rarely an emergency. Seek veterinary care if you notice blood, foul odor, or if your dog has difficulty urinating.

What is preputial discharge in my dog?

Green or yellowish discharge from the prepuce—the sheath covering a dog's penis—is most commonly a substance known as smegma. Smegma is a normal lubricant composed of skin cells, oils, and moisture that helps protect the sensitive tissues. While the color may look concerning, a small amount of discharge is often a normal part of male dog anatomy.

Is green discharge from my dog's penis a medical emergency?

The urgency level for green discharge is typically Low. If your dog is behaving normally, eating well, and not showing signs of pain, this is rarely an emergency. You should seek veterinary advice if you notice the following:

  • Excessive discharge or a foul odor
  • Constant licking of the genital area
  • Visible blood or significant swelling
  • Straining or inability to urinate

How does sending a photo help a vet triage my dog's discharge?

Sharing a clear photograph with a triage professional can be extremely beneficial. A photo allows a professional to perform the following checks:

  • Assess the consistency and volume of the discharge
  • Check the surrounding skin for redness or pustules
  • Distinguish between normal smegma and clinical balanoposthitis (inflammation)
  • Determine the appropriate level of care needed without unnecessary stress

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, a mucopurulent (green) discharge from the prepuce is a common clinical sign of balanoposthitis, an inflammation of the penile or preputial mucosa. While mild balanoposthitis can be present in sexually mature dogs and often resolves spontaneously, more severe cases may be caused by trauma, lacerations, neoplasia, foreign bodies, urinary tract infection, urolithiasis, or phimosis. Veterinary examination, including thorough examination of the penis and prepuce, is warranted to identify underlying predisposing factors if the discharge is profuse or accompanied by other signs such as excessive licking of the prepuce or signs of systemic illness.

Chapter: Urology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1401)

faqs.question: Is green discharge always a sign of infection?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. In many male dogs, a small amount of yellow-green discharge is normal smegma. It only suggests infection if it is accompanied by swelling, redness, or behavioral changes.

faqs.question: Can I clean the area at home?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can gently wipe the area with a soft, damp cloth or a pet-safe wipe. Avoid using harsh soaps, alcohol, or hydrogen peroxide, as these can irritate the sensitive mucous membranes.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a more serious problem?
faqs.answer: Watch for lethargy, loss of appetite, blood in the urine, or an inability to retract the penis into the sheath. These symptoms require a prompt veterinary examination.
